questions.text: An inertial frame of reference is one which satisfies:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An inertial frame of reference is one that satisfies the following conditions: It is not accelerating. It is not rotating. It is not experiencing any external forces. In an inertial frame of reference, Newton's laws of motion hold true. This means that if an object is at rest in an inertial frame of reference, it will remain at rest unless acted upon by a force. Similarly, if an object is moving at a constant velocity in an inertial frame of reference, it will continue to move at that velocity unless acted upon by a force.
Einstein's theory is a broad term that can refer to any of Einstein's theories, but it is most commonly used to refer to his theory of special relativity.

Hertz theory is a theory of electromagnetism that was developed by Heinrich Hertz in the late 19th century. It states that electromagnetic waves are propagated through space as transverse waves and that they travel at the speed of light.

The special theory of relativity is a scientific theory of the relationship between space and time. It was developed by Albert Einstein in 1905, and it is based on two postulates: The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference (that is, frames of reference with no acceleration). The speed of light in a vacuum is the same for all observers, regardless of their relative motion or the motion of the light source.

questions.explanation: An inertial frame of reference is one that satisfies the following conditions: It is not accelerating. It is not rotating. It is not experiencing any external forces. In an inertial frame of reference, Newton's laws of motion hold true. This means that if an object is at rest in an inertial frame of reference, it will remain at rest unless acted upon by a force. Similarly, if an object is moving at a constant velocity in an inertial frame of reference, it will continue to move at that velocity unless acted upon by a force.

options.explanation: The answer decreased is wrong because the speed of light cannot be decreased.
The answer increased is wrong because the speed of light cannot be increased.

The answer maximum is wrong because there is no maximum speed for light.

questions.text: The relativistic length of a moving object will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The answer remain the same is wrong because the length of an object is contracted along the direction of motion when it is moving at relativistic speeds.

The relativistic length of a moving object will be decreased, as seen by an observer moving relative to the object. This is a consequence of the special theory of relativity, which states that the length of an object is contracted along the direction of motion when it is moving at relativistic speeds.
The answer increased is wrong because the length of an object cannot be increased by motion.
The answer doubled is wrong because the length of an object is not doubled by motion.

questions.text: If the rest mass of a particle is zero, then its speed is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the rest mass of a particle is zero, then its speed is speed of light. This is because the rest mass of a particle is a measure of the inertia of the particle, and inertia is a measure of how much resistance a particle has to change its motion. A particle with zero rest mass has no inertia, and therefore it can move at the speed of light.

options.text: Less than the speed of light
options.explanation: The answer less than the speed of light is wrong because a particle with zero rest mass cannot move at a speed less than the speed of light.

options.text: Greater than the speed of light
options.explanation: The answer more than the speed of light is wrong because the speed of light is the maximum speed that any particle can travel.

options.text: Not comparable with speed of light
options.explanation: The answer not comparable to the speed of light is wrong because the speed of light is a well-defined speed, and it is perfectly comparable to the speed of a particle with zero rest mass.

questions.explanation: If the rest mass of a particle is zero, then its speed is the speed of light. This is because the rest mass of a particle is a measure of the inertia of the particle, and inertia is a measure of how much resistance a particle has to change its motion. A particle with zero rest mass has no inertia, and therefore it can move at the speed of light.

questions.text: In special theory of relativity, the law of conservation of energy and law of conservation of mass have been replaced with
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The law of conservation of mass only
options.explanation: This is also not correct, as mass and energy are interchangeable, and mass is also conserved.This is not correct, as mass and energy are interchangeable in special relativity

In the special theory of relativity, Albert Einstein showed that mass (m) and energy (E) are equivalent and can be converted into each other, as expressed by the famous equation:E = mc^2This means that the laws of conservation of energy and conservation of mass are no longer separate, but are combined into a single law: the law of conservation of mass-energy. This law states that the total mass-energy of a closed system remains constant over time.

options.text: law of conservation of energy only

questions.text: The black body always _ radiations:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: a) Emit: This option is true. A black body always emits radiation. When a black body is at a non-zero temperature, it emits thermal radiation in the form of electromagnetic waves, covering a continuous spectrum of wavelengths. This phenomenon is known as black body radiation or thermal radiation.

b) Absorb: This option is also true. A black body absorbs all incident radiation that falls upon it. This is one of the defining characteristics of a black body. It absorbs radiation of all wavelengths and does not reflect or transmit any of it.
c) Both Emit and Absorb: This option is true. A black body both emits and absorbs radiation. It absorbs all incident radiation and emits thermal radiation as described in options (a) and (b).
d) Reflects: This option is not true. A black body does not reflect any radiation; it absorbs all incident radiation without reflection.

questions.explanation: A black body or blackbody is an idealized physical body that absorbs all incident electromagnetic radiation, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence. The name "black body" is given because it absorbs all colors of light. A black body also emits black-body radiation.

questions.text: What is the rest mass of a photon?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Photons, being massless particles, have a rest mass of zero. According to special relativity, photons always travel at the speed of light (c) and have no rest mass. This means that a photon's energy (E) is equal to its momentum (p) multiplied by the speed of light (E = pc), and its rest mass is zero.

options.text: Equal to the mass of electron
options.explanation: The rest mass of an electron is approximately 9.11 × 10^-31 kilograms, which is not the same as the rest mass of a photon.

options.text: Equal to the mass of proton
options.explanation: The rest mass of a proton is approximately 1.67 × 10^-27 kilograms, which is also not the same as the rest mass of a photon
The rest mass of a photon is zero, not infinite

questions.explanation: Photons, being massless particles, have a rest mass of zero. According to special relativity, photons always travel at the speed of light (c) and have no rest mass. This means that a photon's energy (E) is equal to its momentum (p) multiplied by the speed of light (E = pc), and its rest mass is zero.

questions.text: Which light photon has the least momentum?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Red light has the longest wavelength of all visible light, about 700 nanometers. The momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, so the photon with the longest wavelength will have the least momentum. Therefore, the momentum of a red photon is the least of all visible light photons.
Green light has a wavelength of about 550 nanometers. This is shorter than the wavelength of red light, so the momentum of a green photon is greater than the momentum of a red photon.

Yellow light has a wavelength of about 580 nanometers. This is between the wavelengths of red and green light, so the momentum of a yellow photon is between the momentum of a red photon and the momentum of a green photon.
Blue light has a wavelength of about 400 nanometers. This is the shortest wavelength of all visible light, so the momentum of a blue photon is the greatest of all visible light photons.

questions.explanation: The light photon with the least momentum is red. This is because red light has the longest wavelength of all visible light. The momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, so the photon with the longest wavelength will have the least momentum.The momentum of a photon can be calculated using the following equation:p = h/λwhere p is the momentum, h is Planck's constant, and λ is the wavelength.The wavelength of red light is about 700 nanometers, while the wavelength of blue light is about 400 nanometers. This means that the momentum of a red photon is about half the momentum of a blue photon.Therefore, the light photon with the least momentum is red.

questions.text: A material whose K absorption edge is 0.15 Å is irradiated with 0.1 Å X-rays. The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons that are emitted from the K-shell is :
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct. This is the calculated maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons using the difference in energy between the incident X-rays and the K absorption edge.To determine the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons, we need to calculate the energy of the incident X-rays and the energy corresponding to the K absorption edge. The energy of a photon is given by E = hc/λ, where h is Planck's constant and c is the speed of light.The energy of the 0.1 Å X-rays is calculated as:Eincident = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.1 x 10-10 m) = 1.986 x 10-15 J = 124 keVThe energy corresponding to the K absorption edge is:EK = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.15 x 10-10 m) = 1.324 x 10-15 J = 83 keVThe maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is the difference:Ekinetic = Eincident - EK = 124 keV - 83 keV = 41 keVThus, the correct answer is 41 keV. The other options represent incorrect calculations of this energy difference, leading to overestimated or inconsistent values.
Incorrect. This value overestimates the energy difference, leading to an incorrect calculation of kinetic energy.
Incorrect. This value is not consistent with the energy difference based on the given wavelengths.
Incorrect. This value greatly overestimates the kinetic energy and does not match the calculated energy difference.

questions.explanation: To determine the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons, we need to calculate the energy of the incident X-rays and the energy corresponding to the K absorption edge. The energy of a photon is given by E = hc/λ, where h is Planck's constant and c is the speed of light.The energy of the 0.1 Å X-rays is calculated as:Eincident = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.1 x 10-10 m) = 1.986 x 10-15 J = 124 keVThe energy corresponding to the K absorption edge is:EK = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.15 x 10-10 m) = 1.324 x 10-15 J = 83 keVThe maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is the difference:Ekinetic = Eincident - EK = 124 keV - 83 keV = 41 keVThus, the correct answer is 41 keV. The other options represent incorrect calculations of this energy difference, leading to overestimated or inconsistent values.

questions.text: Stopping potentials of 24, 100, 110, 115 kV are measured for photoelectrons emitted from a certain element when it is irradiated with monochromatic X-rays. The element is used as a target in an X-ray tube. The energy of the K line is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: (100−24)×103 eV = 76×103 eVHence, this option is incorrect according to the solution.

Stopping potentials are 24, 100, 110 and 115 kV, i.e. if the electrons are emitted from conduction band maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons would be 115×103 eV. If they are emitted from next inner shell, maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons would be 110×103 eV and so on. for photoelectrons of L- shell it would be 100×103 eV and for K- shell it is 24×103 ev. Therefore, diference between energy of L-shell and K-shell isΔE=EL−KK=(100−24)×103 eV=76×103eV=76KevHence, this option is correct according to the solution.

questions.text: All electrons ejected from a surface by incident light of wavelength 200 nm can be stopped before travelling 1 m in the direction of a uniform electric field of 4N/C. The work function of the surface is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: 4 eV is the maximum kinetic energy that the field can remove (not the work function). If the work function were 4 eV, the photon (6.2 eV) would give at most 2.2 eV KE — which contradicts the stopping condition. So this option confuses KE with work function.
6.2 eV is the photon energy itself (from 200 nm). If the work function were 6.2 eV, no electrons would be emitted. That contradicts the premise that electrons are ejected and then stopped within 1 m.

2.0 eV is too small. If the work function were 2.0 eV, the maximum kinetic energy would be 6.2−2.0=4.2 eV, which is greater than the 4 eV the field can stop — some electrons would travel beyond 1 m. That contradicts the statement that all electrons are stopped before 1 m.

This value makes the maximum possible kinetic energy 6.2−2.2=4.0 eV, exactly the energy the field (4 V over 1 m) can remove. Thus all emitted electrons can be stopped within 1 m, matching the experiment.

Let's first calculate the energy of the first radiation.Energy = hv/λ = (6.63x10-34 x 3x108)/ 400x10-9 = 4.9725x10-19 J = 4.9725x10-19 / 1.6 x10-19 = 3.1 evThe energy of the second radiationEnergy = hv/λ = (6.63x10-34 x 3x108)/ 310x10-9 = 6.416x10-19 J= 6.416x10-19/1.6x10-19= 4 evK.E = Energy - Φ = 3.1 - ΦThe second radiation will have twice K.E2K.E = 4 - ΦK.E = (4 - Φ)/2K.E = K.E(4 - Φ) / 2 = 3.1 - Φ4 - Φ = 2(3.1 - Φ)4 - Φ = 6.2 - 2Φ-Φ + 2Φ = 6.2 - 4Φ = 2.2 ev
As per the calculation, the correct answer is 2.2 ev. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: A metallic surface ejects photoelectrons when hit by green light but none when hit by yellow light. Will the electrons be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option states that electrons will be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light. However, based on the information given, the surface only ejects photoelectrons when hit by green light and does not eject photoelectrons when hit by yellow light. There is no direct information about the behavior of the surface when hit by red light. Therefore, it is not certain whether electrons will be ejected when the surface is hit by red light. This option is not necessarily true.

options.text: Yes, if the red beam is sufficiently intense
options.explanation: This option suggests that electrons will be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light, but the intensity of the red light needs to be sufficiently high. The intensity of light affects the number of photons incident on the surface and, therefore, the number of ejected photoelectrons. If the red beam is intense enough, it can provide sufficient energy to eject photoelectrons from the metallic surface. Therefore, this option is plausible.

options.text: Yes, if the red beam is allowed to fall for sufficient duration
options.explanation: This option suggests that electrons will be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light, but the duration of exposure to the red light needs to be sufficient. The duration of exposure determines the total energy transferred to the surface, which can lead to the ejection of photoelectrons. If the red beam falls on the surface for a sufficient period, the surface can absorb enough energy to eject photoelectrons. Therefore, this option is also plausible.

This option states that electrons will not be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light. Based on the given information that the surface ejects photoelectrons when hit by green light but none when hit by yellow light, we can infer that the work function of the metallic surface is higher than the energy of yellow light but lower than the energy of green light. If the red light has lower energy than green light, it is unlikely to have sufficient energy to overcome the work function and eject photoelectrons. Therefore, this option is likely the correct answer.

questions.text: Light of frequency 1.5 times the threshold frequency is incident on a photosensitive material. Photoelectric current is emitted. If the frequency of light is halved and intensity is doubled, the photoelectric current becomes:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the photoelectric current becomes four times the original current when the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled. However, this is not necessarily true based on the given information. To determine the actual change in the current, we need to consider the relationship between the frequency, intensity, and photoelectric current.

This option suggests that the photoelectric current becomes two times the original current when the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled. Again, this is not necessarily true based on the given information.

This option suggests that the photoelectric current becomes half the original current when the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled. This is the correct answer. According to the photoelectric effect, the current depends on the intensity of the incident light, and doubling the intensity can potentially double the current. However, halving the frequency may reduce the current. Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that the photoelectric current would be half the original current in this scenario.

This option suggests that the photoelectric current becomes zero when the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled. Based on the given information, this is not necessarily true. The photoelectric current is emitted when light of frequency 1.5 times the threshold frequency is incident on the photosensitive material. Halving the frequency may reduce the current, but it does not guarantee a complete elimination of the current.

questions.text: λ is proportional to?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is incorrect as per the given explanation.

options.text: 1/√ E for both photons and particles
options.explanation: It is incorrect as per the given explanation.

options.text: 1/E for photons and 1/√ E for particles
options.explanation: The wavelength of a photon is inversely proportional to its energy, but for particles, the wavelength is inversely proportional to the square root of its energy. This is because the energy of a photon is given by the following equation: E = hν where: h is Planck's constant ν is the frequency of the photon The frequency of a photon is given by the following equation: ν = c/λ where: c is the speed of light λ is the wavelength of the photon Substituting the equation for ν into the equation for E, we get: E = h(c/λ) This means that E is inversely proportional to λ. For particles, the wavelength is given by the following equation: λ = h/p where: h is Planck's constant p is the momentum of the particle The momentum of a particle is given by the following equation: p = mv where: m is the mass of the particle v is the velocity of the particle Substituting the equation for p into the equation for λ, we get: λ = h/mv This means that λ is inversely proportional to the square root of E.

For particles, the wavelength is given by the following equation: λ = h/p where: h is Planck's constant p is the momentum of the particle The momentum of a particle is given by the following equation: p = mv where: m is the mass of the particle v is the velocity of the particle Substituting the equation for p into the equation for λ, we get: λ = h/mv This means that λ is inversely proportional to the square root of E.

questions.text: Which of the following graphs correcty represents the variation of particle momentum with associated de broglie wavelength?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is incorrect according to the solution.
It is incorrect according to the solution.
It is incorrect according to the solution.

The inversely proportional graph correctly represents the variation of particle momentum with associated de Broglie wavelength. The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by the following equation:λ = h/p where:λ is the de Broglie wavelengthh is Planck's constantp is the momentum of the particleThis means that the wavelength is inversely proportional to the momentum. In other words, as the momentum of the particle increases, the wavelength decreases. The graph of momentum versus wavelength is therefore an inverse graph.Here is an example of an inverse graph of momentum versus wavelength:Momentum (p) is inversly proportional to Wavelength (λ)10 p | 1/10 λ20 p | 1/20 λ30 p | 1/30 λAs you can see, as the momentum of the particle increases, the wavelength decreases. The inverse relationship between momentum and wavelength is a fundamental property of quantum mechanics. It is responsible for the wave-particle duality of matter, which is the property of matter to exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties.

The inverse relationship between momentum and wavelength is a fundamental property of quantum mechanics. It is responsible for the wave-particle duality of matter, which is the property of matter to exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties.

Yes, a photon is considered to have both wavelength and frequency. This is because a photon is a quantum of light, and light is a wave. The wavelength of a photon is the distance between two consecutive peaks of the wave, and the frequency of a photon is the number of waves that pass a point in a given time. The wavelength of a photon is given by the following equation:λ = h/p where:λ is the wavelength of the photonh is Planck's constantp is the momentum of the photonThe frequency of a photon is given by the following equation:ν = c/λ where:ν is the frequency of the photon As you can see, the wavelength and frequency of a photon are inversely proportional. This means that as the wavelength of a photon increases, the frequency decreases, and vice versa.

Yes, a photon is considered to have momentum. This is because photons carry energy, and energy is equivalent to momentum. The momentum of a photon is given by the following equation: p = h/λ where:p is the momentum of the photonh is Planck's constantλ is the wavelength of the photonThe momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength. This means that photons with shorter wavelengths have higher momenta.

questions.text: The photoelectric effect depends upon the photon's
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The pressure of the metal does not affect the photoelectric effect. This is because the pressure of the metal does not affect the energy of the photons that hit it.

The temperature of the metal can affect the photoelectric effect, but only at very low temperatures. This is because, at very low temperatures, the electrons in the metal can have more energy, which means that they can be emitted by photons of lower frequency. the temperature of the photons does not affect.

The intensity of the light only affects the number of electrons emitted. This is because the intensity of light is a measure of the number of photons per unit area, and the more photons that hit the metal, the more electrons will be emitted.
The frequency of the light determines the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. This is because the energy of a photon is proportional to its frequency.

questions.explanation: The frequency of the light determines the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. This is because the energy of a photon is proportional to its frequency.

questions.text: Einstein's Photoelectric equation is Ek =hf -Φ in this equation Ek refers to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: K.E of all the emitted electrons
options.explanation: This option suggests that Ek represents the kinetic energy (K.E) of all the emitted electrons. It implies that the equation calculates the total kinetic energy of all the electrons emitted during the photoelectric effect. However, this interpretation is incorrect.

This option proposes that Ek refers to the mean (average) kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. It suggests that the equation calculates the average kinetic energy of the electrons involved in the photoelectric effect. However, this is not what Ek represents in the context of the photoelectric equation.

This option correctly identifies that Ek represents the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron. In the photoelectric effect, when a photon with energy hf (where h is Planck's constant and f is the frequency of the incident light) is incident on a metal surface, it can liberate electrons from the metal if the energy of the photon is greater than the work function of the metal (Φ). The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron occurs when the incident photon's energy is fully used to liberate the electron and overcome the work function.

This option suggests that Ek refers to the minimum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. However, the photoelectric equation does not describe the minimum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. The minimum kinetic energy would be zero, which corresponds to the threshold energy required to overcome the work function and liberate an electron, but it does not provide any additional information about the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron.

questions.explanation: The photoelectric equation K = hf - Φ is used to calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron during the photoelectric effect, where Ek represents this maximum kinetic energy.

questions.text: Monochromatic light of frequency f1 is incident on a photocell and the stopping potential is found to be V1. What is the new stopping potential of the cell if it is radiated by monochromatic light of frequency f2?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The work function 𝜙ϕ is a property of the metal and does not change.Stopping potential depends linearly on frequency. Increasing frequency increases the stopping potential.
